foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Washington established the foundational principles of American foreign policy: neutrality, avoidance of permanent alliances, and assertion of executive authority in diplomacy. His administration navigated intense European conflict, negotiated key treaties that stabilized borders and commerce, and asserted national sovereignty against foreign interference. Washington’s decisions shaped the constitutional balance between Congress and the executive in foreign affairs and set precedents that guided U.S. diplomacy for generations.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Jay Treaty with Great Britain (1794)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty resolved lingering post‑Revolutionary War disputes, secured British evacuation of frontier forts, and established commercial arrangements. Though controversial domestically, it prevented war with Britain and stabilized the young nation’s economy.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Pinckney’s Treaty with Spain (1795)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty secured American navigation rights on the Mississippi River, the right of deposit at New Orleans, and a clear boundary with Spanish Florida, strengthening western expansion and trade.

militaryActions.title: Neutrality in the French Revolutionary Wars
militaryActions.summary: Washington declared neutrality in 1793 to avoid entanglement in European conflicts, resisting pressure to support France. The policy asserted executive authority and shaped early American diplomacy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Proclamation of Neutrality (1793)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Washington formally declared U.S. neutrality in the war between Britain and France, asserting executive control over foreign policy and avoiding entangling alliances.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Citizen Genêt Affair
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: French envoy Edmond‑Charles Genêt attempted to recruit American support for France in violation of U.S. neutrality. Washington demanded his recall, reinforcing executive authority over foreign envoys.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Adams’s foreign policy was dominated by escalating tensions with France, culminating in the Quasi‑War. He strengthened the U.S. Navy, resisted pressure for full‑scale war, and ultimately negotiated peace. His diplomacy preserved American neutrality and strengthened executive authority, though domestic political divisions intensified.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Convention of 1800 (Treaty of Mortefontaine)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty ended the Quasi‑War with France, restored commercial relations, and terminated the 1778 Franco‑American alliance, reaffirming U.S. neutrality.

militaryActions.title: Quasi‑War with France (1798–1800)
militaryActions.summary: An undeclared naval conflict in the Caribbean and Atlantic, fought over French seizures of American ships. Adams expanded the Navy and authorized limited hostilities.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: XYZ Affair
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: French agents demanded bribes from American diplomats, sparking national outrage and leading to the Quasi‑War.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Jefferson’s foreign policy balanced territorial expansion, commercial protection, and avoidance of war. His administration achieved the Louisiana Purchase, confronted Barbary piracy, and struggled with British and French interference in American trade. Jefferson favored economic coercion over military conflict, though his embargo policies proved deeply unpopular.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Louisiana Purchase Treaty (1803)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Jefferson acquired the Louisiana Territory from France, doubling U.S. territory and securing control of the Mississippi River.

militaryActions.title: First Barbary War (1801–1805)
militaryActions.summary: Jefferson deployed naval forces against Barbary states to stop piracy and protect American commerce in the Mediterranean.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Embargo Act of 1807
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Jefferson halted all American exports to pressure Britain and France to respect U.S. neutrality. The policy backfired economically and politically.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Madison’s foreign policy was dominated by escalating tensions with Britain over maritime rights, impressment, and frontier conflict. His administration led the nation into the War of 1812, defended national sovereignty, and emerged with strengthened American identity and diplomatic standing.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Treaty of Ghent (1814)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty ended the War of 1812, restored prewar boundaries, and established commissions to resolve future disputes.

militaryActions.title: War of 1812
militaryActions.summary: A conflict with Britain over maritime rights, impressment, and frontier tensions. The war tested U.S. military capacity and national unity.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: British Impressment of American Sailors
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: British naval forces seized American sailors, a major cause of the War of 1812.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Monroe’s foreign policy marked the emergence of the United States as a hemispheric power. His administration secured major territorial agreements, strengthened U.S. borders, and articulated the Monroe Doctrine, asserting opposition to European colonization in the Americas. Monroe presided over a period of diplomatic confidence and expanding national influence.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Adams–Onís Treaty (1819)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Spain ceded Florida to the United States and established a clear boundary between U.S. and Spanish territories in the West.

militaryActions.title: Seminole Conflicts and Jackson’s Florida Campaign
militaryActions.summary: U.S. forces under Andrew Jackson conducted unauthorized incursions into Spanish Florida, pressuring Spain to negotiate the Adams–Onís Treaty.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Monroe Doctrine (1823)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Monroe declared that the Western Hemisphere was closed to future European colonization and that the U.S. would oppose European interference in the Americas. The doctrine became a cornerstone of U.S. foreign policy.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: John Quincy Adams, one of the most skilled diplomats in American history, pursued a foreign policy focused on expanding U.S. commercial rights, strengthening national sovereignty, and promoting peaceful engagement. His presidency emphasized arbitration, trade liberalization, and the defense of neutral rights. Although he faced domestic political opposition, Adams continued the diplomatic vision he had shaped as Monroe’s Secretary of State, promoting American influence without military conflict.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Commercial Treaties with Prussia, Denmark, and the Hanseatic Cities
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Adams negotiated several commercial agreements expanding American trade access in Europe. These treaties strengthened U.S. commercial rights and reflected his commitment to peaceful economic expansion.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Defense of the Monroe Doctrine
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Adams continued to uphold the Monroe Doctrine’s principles, resisting European interference in the Western Hemisphere and reinforcing U.S. diplomatic leadership.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Jackson’s foreign policy emphasized assertive nationalism, expansion of American trade, and defense of U.S. sovereignty. He confronted European powers diplomatically, secured long‑standing claims, and pursued aggressive stances toward Native nations and Latin American states. Jackson’s administration strengthened the presidency’s role in foreign affairs and expanded American influence abroad.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Trade Agreements with Britain and France
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Jackson negotiated reciprocal trade agreements that expanded American commercial access, particularly in the West Indies.

militaryActions.title: Use of Force in Latin American Boundary Disputes
militaryActions.summary: Jackson threatened military action to defend U.S. claims and protect American citizens in Latin America, reinforcing U.S. regional influence.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: French Indemnity Crisis (1834–1836)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: France delayed payment of agreed‑upon reparations for seized American ships. Jackson threatened retaliation, prompting France to honor the treaty.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Van Buren maintained a cautious foreign policy focused on avoiding war and preserving neutrality during a period of global instability. He resisted domestic pressure to annex Texas, managed tensions with Britain, and upheld diplomatic restraint. His administration prioritized stability and peaceful resolution of disputes.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Caroline Affair (1837–1838)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: A British attack on the American steamer Caroline during Canadian rebellions sparked diplomatic tension. Van Buren pursued a peaceful resolution, avoiding war with Britain.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Rejection of Texas Annexation
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Van Buren opposed annexing Texas to avoid war with Mexico and inflaming sectional tensions, maintaining diplomatic restraint.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Harrison’s presidency lasted only one month, leaving no meaningful foreign‑policy legacy. His diplomatic philosophy aligned with Whig principles favoring congressional authority and cautious engagement abroad.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: No Significant Foreign Policy Actions
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Harrison died before implementing any foreign‑policy initiatives or responding to international developments.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Tyler’s foreign policy emphasized territorial expansion, commercial diplomacy, and assertive defense of American interests. His administration advanced the annexation of Texas, strengthened Pacific trade, and resolved long‑standing disputes with Britain. Tyler expanded U.S. influence despite political isolation at home.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Webster–Ashburton Treaty (1842)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty resolved U.S.–British border disputes in Maine and the Great Lakes region, reduced tensions, and strengthened Anglo‑American relations.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Texas Annexation Efforts
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Tyler aggressively pursued the annexation of Texas, laying the groundwork for its admission shortly after he left office.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Polk’s foreign policy was driven by expansionism and the doctrine of Manifest Destiny. His administration achieved major territorial gains through diplomacy and war, including the Oregon boundary settlement and the Mexican–American War. Polk strengthened American influence on the continent, expanded U.S. borders to the Pacific, and reshaped the geopolitical landscape of North America.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Oregon Treaty (1846)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty peacefully resolved the U.S.–British dispute over the Oregon Territory, establishing the 49th parallel as the boundary and securing American access to the Pacific Northwest.

militaryActions.title: Mexican–American War (1846–1848)
militaryActions.summary: Polk initiated war with Mexico over disputed territory in Texas and ambitions for western expansion. The conflict resulted in major U.S. territorial gains, including California and the Southwest.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Annexation of Texas (1845)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Polk supported and finalized the annexation of Texas, a key factor leading to war with Mexico.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Taylor’s foreign policy was shaped by the aftermath of the Mexican–American War and rising sectional tensions over slavery in newly acquired territories. Although his presidency was short, Taylor resisted Southern efforts to expand slavery through foreign intervention and opposed aggressive expansionist schemes.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Opposition to Cuban Annexation Schemes
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Taylor rejected Southern attempts to acquire Cuba from Spain, resisting expansionist pressures tied to slavery.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Fillmore’s foreign policy emphasized opening new markets, expanding Pacific trade, and asserting American influence abroad. His administration pursued commercial diplomacy, strengthened naval presence, and laid groundwork for future expansion into Asia.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Perry Expedition to Japan (1852–1853)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Fillmore authorized Commodore Matthew Perry’s mission to open Japan to American trade, initiating a major shift in East Asian diplomacy.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Pierce pursued an aggressive expansionist foreign policy aligned with Southern interests. His administration sought new slaveholding territories, confronted European powers in the Caribbean, and expanded American commercial influence. Pierce’s diplomacy deepened sectional tensions and contributed to the coming crisis over slavery.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Gadsden Purchase (1853)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Pierce acquired land from Mexico to facilitate a southern transcontinental railroad route, expanding U.S. territory in the Southwest.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Ostend Manifesto (1854)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Pierce’s diplomats argued that the U.S. should purchase Cuba from Spain and consider war if Spain refused. The manifesto sparked national controversy and heightened sectional divisions.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Buchanan’s foreign policy was marked by indecision and sectional bias. He pursued expansionist goals aligned with Southern interests, attempted to acquire Cuba, and failed to prevent escalating tensions with Britain and Central American states. His administration’s diplomatic missteps contributed to national instability on the eve of the Civil War.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Utah Expedition (1857–1858)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Although primarily a domestic conflict, Buchanan’s deployment of federal troops to Utah had international implications due to British interest in the region.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Continued Attempts to Acquire Cuba
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Buchanan supported efforts to purchase Cuba from Spain, continuing the expansionist agenda of previous administrations.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Lincoln’s foreign policy focused on preventing European recognition of the Confederacy, maintaining Union diplomatic legitimacy, and navigating global reactions to the Civil War. His administration managed crises with Britain and France, asserted blockade authority, and used diplomacy to isolate the Confederacy internationally. Lincoln’s foreign policy preserved the Union’s standing and prevented foreign intervention that could have altered the war’s outcome.

militaryActions.title: Union Naval Blockade of the Confederacy
militaryActions.summary: Lincoln declared a blockade of Southern ports, asserting belligerent rights under international law. The blockade was central to Union strategy and required careful diplomacy to avoid conflict with neutral powers.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Trent Affair (1861)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Union forces seized two Confederate envoys from a British ship, provoking a diplomatic crisis. Lincoln released the envoys to avoid war with Britain, famously saying, 'One war at a time.'

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Prevention of European Recognition of the Confederacy
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Lincoln and Secretary of State Seward worked tirelessly to prevent Britain and France from recognizing the Confederacy, using diplomacy, economic leverage, and the Emancipation Proclamation.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Johnson’s foreign policy focused on maintaining the Monroe Doctrine, resisting European influence in the Americas, and continuing territorial expansion. His administration confronted French intervention in Mexico, pursued the purchase of Alaska, and maintained assertive diplomatic positions despite domestic political turmoil.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Alaska Purchase Treaty (1867)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Johnson’s administration negotiated the purchase of Alaska from Russia, expanding U.S. territory and securing strategic advantages in the North Pacific.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: End of French Intervention in Mexico
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Johnson pressured France to withdraw its forces from Mexico, supporting the restoration of Mexican sovereignty under the Monroe Doctrine.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Grant’s foreign policy emphasized peaceful arbitration, hemispheric stability, and expansion of American influence. His administration pursued annexation of the Dominican Republic, resolved major disputes with Britain through arbitration, and strengthened U.S. standing as a diplomatic power.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Treaty of Washington (1871)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty resolved Civil War–era disputes with Britain, including the Alabama Claims, through international arbitration. It marked a major step in peaceful dispute resolution.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Attempted Annexation of the Dominican Republic
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Grant sought to annex the Dominican Republic for strategic and economic reasons, but the Senate rejected the proposal.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Hayes pursued a restrained foreign policy focused on trade expansion, protection of American commercial interests, and maintaining stability in Latin America and Asia. His administration strengthened relations with China and resisted aggressive expansionism.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Burlingame Treaty Modifications
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Hayes supported renegotiation of U.S.–China agreements to address immigration tensions while preserving friendly relations.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Opposition to Chinese Exclusion Legislation
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Hayes vetoed early attempts to restrict Chinese immigration, citing treaty obligations and diplomatic concerns.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Garfield’s foreign policy emphasized modernization of the Navy, protection of American commercial interests, and strengthening U.S. influence in Latin America. His presidency was cut short by assassination, limiting his ability to implement major initiatives.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Support for Pan‑American Diplomacy
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Garfield planned to convene a Pan‑American conference to promote hemispheric cooperation, but his assassination prevented its realization.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Arthur’s foreign policy emphasized modernization, commercial expansion, and strengthening America’s presence in the Pacific. His administration pursued naval reform, negotiated trade agreements, and supported early Pan‑American cooperation. Arthur avoided major conflicts while laying groundwork for a more assertive U.S. role abroad.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Commercial Treaty with Korea (1883)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Arthur expanded diplomatic and commercial relations with Korea, building on earlier negotiations and opening new markets in East Asia.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Support for Naval Modernization
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Arthur backed the creation of a modern steel navy, strengthening U.S. maritime power and enabling future global engagement.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Cleveland pursued a cautious foreign policy emphasizing neutrality, anti‑imperialism, and respect for international law. He resisted expansionist pressures, opposed annexation of Hawaii, and defended U.S. interests through diplomacy rather than force. His administration strengthened the legal and moral foundations of American foreign policy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Opposition to Hawaiian Annexation (1893)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Cleveland opposed the annexation of Hawaii after a U.S.-backed coup, arguing the overthrow was illegitimate and demanding restoration of the Hawaiian monarchy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Venezuelan Boundary Dispute (1895)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Cleveland invoked the Monroe Doctrine to demand arbitration of a British–Venezuelan border dispute, asserting U.S. authority in the Western Hemisphere.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Harrison’s foreign policy expanded American commercial influence, strengthened the Navy, and promoted Pan‑American cooperation. His administration supported early moves toward overseas expansion and pursued assertive diplomacy in Latin America and the Pacific.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Pan‑American Conference Agreements (1889–1890)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Harrison supported the first Pan‑American Conference, which promoted hemispheric cooperation and trade.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Expansion of the Modern Navy
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Harrison accelerated naval modernization, enabling the U.S. to project power globally in the coming decades.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Cleveland’s second term continued his anti‑imperialist approach, resisting expansionist pressures and emphasizing legalistic diplomacy. He confronted crises in Latin America and the Pacific while maintaining a cautious, principled foreign policy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Renewed Opposition to Hawaiian Annexation
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Cleveland again opposed annexation efforts, condemning the overthrow of Queen Liliʻuokalani and rejecting imperialist ambitions.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Venezuelan Boundary Arbitration Enforcement
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Cleveland continued to push Britain toward arbitration, reinforcing U.S. authority under the Monroe Doctrine.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: McKinley’s foreign policy marked a turning point as the United States emerged as an imperial power. His administration led the nation into the Spanish–American War, acquired overseas territories, expanded Pacific influence, and embraced a more assertive global role. McKinley reshaped American foreign policy for the 20th century.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Treaty of Paris (1898)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty ended the Spanish–American War and ceded Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines to the United States, marking the beginning of American overseas empire.

militaryActions.title: Spanish–American War (1898)
militaryActions.summary: McKinley led the U.S. into war with Spain over Cuba, resulting in decisive American victory and major territorial acquisitions.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Annexation of Hawaii (1898)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: McKinley supported and signed the annexation of Hawaii, expanding U.S. strategic presence in the Pacific.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Roosevelt transformed American foreign policy into an assertive, globally engaged doctrine. His administration expanded U.S. influence through the Roosevelt Corollary, oversaw construction of the Panama Canal, strengthened the Navy, and intervened in Latin America to stabilize governments and protect American interests. Roosevelt’s diplomacy blended negotiation with the credible threat of force, establishing the United States as a major world power.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Hay–Bunau-Varilla Treaty (1903)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: The treaty granted the United States rights to build and control the Panama Canal Zone, enabling construction of one of the most important strategic waterways in the world.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Treaty of Portsmouth (1905)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Roosevelt mediated the end of the Russo‑Japanese War, earning the Nobel Peace Prize and elevating U.S. diplomatic prestige.

militaryActions.title: Interventions in the Caribbean and Central America
militaryActions.summary: Roosevelt deployed U.S. forces to stabilize governments in Cuba, the Dominican Republic, and other regions under the Roosevelt Corollary.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine (1904)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Roosevelt asserted the U.S. right to intervene in Latin American nations to maintain stability and prevent European involvement.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Taft’s foreign policy, known as Dollar Diplomacy, emphasized promoting American economic interests abroad, particularly in Latin America and East Asia. His administration encouraged U.S. investment to stabilize foreign governments and expand American influence, while avoiding large‑scale military intervention.

treatiesNegotiated.title: U.S.–Nicaragua Agreements
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Taft negotiated financial and political agreements to stabilize Nicaragua and expand American economic influence.

militaryActions.title: Intervention in Nicaragua (1912)
militaryActions.summary: Taft deployed Marines to support a friendly government and protect American financial interests.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Dollar Diplomacy
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Taft used American economic power to influence foreign governments, particularly in Latin America and China.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Wilson’s foreign policy combined moral idealism with military intervention. His administration entered World War I, promoted self‑determination, and advanced the League of Nations. Wilson intervened frequently in Latin America while attempting to reshape global diplomacy through his Fourteen Points.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Treaty of Versailles (1919)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Wilson helped negotiate the treaty ending World War I and championed the League of Nations, though the U.S. Senate refused to ratify it.

militaryActions.title: U.S. Entry into World War I (1917)
militaryActions.summary: Wilson led the United States into WWI to defend democracy and international order, dramatically expanding American global involvement.

militaryActions.title: Interventions in Mexico and the Caribbean
militaryActions.summary: Wilson deployed forces to Mexico, Haiti, and the Dominican Republic to influence political outcomes and protect U.S. interests.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Fourteen Points (1918)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Wilson outlined principles for postwar peace, including self‑determination, free trade, and collective security.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Harding pursued a foreign policy focused on disarmament, economic recovery, and returning to pre‑war normalcy. His administration promoted naval arms reduction, stabilized relations with former wartime allies, and avoided entangling commitments.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Washington Naval Treaty (1922)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Harding hosted the Washington Naval Conference, resulting in treaties limiting naval armaments among major powers.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Return to Isolationist Tendencies
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Harding rejected U.S. membership in the League of Nations and emphasized limited international commitments.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Coolidge maintained a restrained foreign policy focused on economic diplomacy, debt repayment, and peace initiatives. His administration supported international agreements to reduce conflict while avoiding binding commitments. Coolidge emphasized stability and financial responsibility in global affairs.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Kellogg–Briand Pact (1928)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Coolidge supported the pact renouncing war as a tool of national policy, a major symbolic step toward international peace.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Support for Dawes Plan (1924)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Coolidge backed the Dawes Plan to stabilize German reparations and European economies after World War I.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Hoover’s foreign policy emphasized economic diplomacy, non‑intervention in Latin America, and efforts to stabilize the global financial system during the Great Depression. His administration promoted peaceful conflict resolution, reduced military involvement abroad, and attempted to ease international tensions through debt moratoriums and arms limitations.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Hoover Moratorium on War Debts (1931)
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Hoover proposed a one‑year suspension of World War I reparations and intergovernmental debts to stabilize the global economy during the Depression.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Good Neighbor Policy Foundations
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Hoover reduced U.S. military involvement in Latin America, withdrawing troops from Nicaragua and Haiti and laying groundwork for Roosevelt’s Good Neighbor Policy.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Roosevelt’s foreign policy reshaped the global role of the United States. His administration confronted the rise of fascism, provided critical support to Allied nations, and ultimately led the U.S. into World War II. FDR built the foundations of the postwar international order through the United Nations, global economic institutions, and long‑term alliances.

treatiesNegotiated.title: United Nations Declaration (1942)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Roosevelt helped craft the declaration that united Allied nations in World War II and laid the foundation for the postwar United Nations.

militaryActions.title: World War II (1941–1945)
militaryActions.summary: After the attack on Pearl Harbor, Roosevelt led the U.S. into WWII, coordinating global strategy with Allied leaders and mobilizing the nation for total war.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Lend‑Lease Act (1941)
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Roosevelt provided massive military and economic aid to Allied nations, strengthening their resistance to Axis powers before U.S. entry into WWII.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Atlantic Charter (1941)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Roosevelt and Churchill outlined principles for the postwar world, including self‑determination, free trade, and collective security.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Truman’s foreign policy established the framework of the Cold War. His administration implemented containment, launched the Marshall Plan, created NATO, and authorized U.S. involvement in the Korean War. Truman’s decisions shaped global alliances and defined American leadership in the postwar world.

treatiesNegotiated.title: North Atlantic Treaty (1949)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Truman helped create NATO, a collective defense alliance that became the cornerstone of Western security during the Cold War.

militaryActions.title: Korean War (1950–1953)
militaryActions.summary: Truman committed U.S. forces to defend South Korea under UN authority, marking the first major military conflict of the Cold War.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Marshall Plan (1948)
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Truman implemented massive economic aid to rebuild Western Europe, strengthen democracies, and counter Soviet influence.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Truman Doctrine (1947)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Truman pledged U.S. support for nations resisting communism, establishing the policy of containment.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Eisenhower’s foreign policy emphasized containment of communism, nuclear deterrence, and strategic alliances. His administration expanded Cold War commitments, used covert operations to influence foreign governments, and balanced military strength with efforts to avoid direct conflict with the Soviet Union.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Southeast Asia Treaty Organization (SEATO) (1954)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Eisenhower helped establish SEATO to counter communist expansion in Southeast Asia.

militaryActions.title: Intervention in Lebanon (1958)
militaryActions.summary: Eisenhower deployed U.S. forces to Lebanon to stabilize the government and prevent perceived communist influence.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Eisenhower Doctrine Aid Programs
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Eisenhower provided military and economic aid to Middle Eastern nations resisting communist influence.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: CIA Operations in Iran and Guatemala
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Eisenhower authorized covert operations to overthrow governments in Iran (1953) and Guatemala (1954), expanding U.S. Cold War interventionism.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Kennedy’s foreign policy was defined by Cold War crises, nuclear brinkmanship, and efforts to promote development in the Global South. His administration confronted the Bay of Pigs, Cuban Missile Crisis, and Berlin tensions while expanding foreign aid and launching the Peace Corps.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Limited Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Kennedy negotiated the first major arms control agreement of the Cold War, banning atmospheric nuclear tests.

militaryActions.title: Bay of Pigs Invasion (1961)
militaryActions.summary: Kennedy approved a failed CIA‑backed invasion of Cuba by anti‑Castro exiles, damaging U.S. credibility.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Peace Corps (1961)
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Kennedy created the Peace Corps to promote development and strengthen U.S. soft power abroad.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Cuban Missile Crisis (1962)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Kennedy confronted the Soviet Union over nuclear missiles in Cuba, bringing the world to the brink of nuclear war before negotiating a peaceful resolution.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Johnson’s foreign policy was dominated by the Vietnam War, which escalated dramatically under his administration. While he expanded U.S. involvement in Southeast Asia, he also pursued diplomatic initiatives, strengthened alliances, and advanced global development programs. Johnson’s foreign policy legacy is defined by the tension between Great Society ambitions at home and the burdens of Cold War conflict abroad.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Outer Space Treaty (1967)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Johnson supported the treaty banning nuclear weapons in space and establishing peaceful use of outer space, a major Cold War arms‑control achievement.

militaryActions.title: Vietnam War Escalation (1964–1968)
militaryActions.summary: Johnson dramatically increased U.S. troop levels in Vietnam following the Gulf of Tonkin incident, committing the nation to a prolonged conflict.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Alliance for Progress Expansion
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Johnson continued Kennedy’s Latin American development initiative, providing economic aid to counter communist influence.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Congress authorized Johnson to use military force in Southeast Asia, effectively granting broad war powers without a formal declaration.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Nixon’s foreign policy reshaped global geopolitics through détente with the Soviet Union, opening relations with China, and ending direct U.S. involvement in Vietnam. His administration emphasized realpolitik, strategic arms control, and a shift toward shared defense responsibilities with allies.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT I) (1972)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Nixon negotiated the first major nuclear arms‑control agreement with the Soviet Union, limiting strategic missile systems.

militaryActions.title: Cambodia and Laos Bombing Campaigns
militaryActions.summary: Nixon expanded the Vietnam War into neighboring countries to disrupt North Vietnamese supply lines.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Opening to China (1972)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Nixon’s visit to China normalized relations and shifted Cold War dynamics, counterbalancing Soviet influence.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Ford’s foreign policy focused on maintaining détente, managing the aftermath of the Vietnam War, and stabilizing relations with allies. His administration continued arms‑control negotiations, responded to global crises, and worked to restore confidence in U.S. leadership after Watergate.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Helsinki Accords (1975)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Ford signed the Helsinki Accords, promoting human rights and cooperation between Western and Eastern bloc nations.

militaryActions.title: Mayagüez Incident (1975)
militaryActions.summary: Ford authorized a rescue mission after Cambodian forces seized the SS Mayagüez, resulting in the recovery of the ship and crew.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Final Withdrawal from Vietnam (1975)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Ford oversaw the evacuation of U.S. personnel and allies during the fall of Saigon, marking the end of American involvement in Vietnam.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Carter’s foreign policy emphasized human rights, diplomacy, and peaceful conflict resolution. His administration achieved major breakthroughs in the Middle East, normalized relations with China, and pursued arms‑control agreements. However, the Iranian Revolution and hostage crisis overshadowed many of his accomplishments.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Camp David Accords (1978)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Carter brokered peace between Egypt and Israel, leading to the first Arab‑Israeli peace treaty.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Panama Canal Treaties Implementation
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Carter implemented treaties transferring control of the Panama Canal to Panama, improving hemispheric relations.

militaryActions.title: Failed Iran Hostage Rescue Mission (1980)
militaryActions.summary: Carter authorized Operation Eagle Claw to rescue American hostages in Tehran, but the mission failed due to mechanical and weather issues.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Global Human Rights Assistance
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Carter expanded foreign aid programs tied to human rights performance, reshaping U.S. development policy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Iran Hostage Crisis (1979–1981)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Iranian militants seized the U.S. embassy in Tehran, holding 52 Americans hostage for 444 days, severely damaging Carter’s presidency.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Reagan’s foreign policy sought to confront and roll back Soviet influence through military buildup, ideological pressure, and support for anti‑communist movements. His administration escalated Cold War tensions before negotiating major arms‑control agreements that helped end the conflict. Reagan’s foreign policy reshaped global power dynamics and accelerated the decline of the Soviet Union.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Intermediate‑Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty (1987)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Reagan and Gorbachev signed the INF Treaty, eliminating an entire class of nuclear missiles and marking a major step toward ending the Cold War.

militaryActions.title: Invasion of Grenada (1983)
militaryActions.summary: Reagan ordered U.S. forces to invade Grenada to overthrow a Marxist government and protect American citizens.

militaryActions.title: Bombing of Libya (1986)
militaryActions.summary: Reagan authorized airstrikes against Libya in response to terrorist attacks linked to Muammar Gaddafi.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Support for Anti‑Communist Movements
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Reagan provided aid to anti‑communist groups in Afghanistan, Nicaragua, and Angola as part of the Reagan Doctrine.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Reagan Doctrine
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Reagan expanded U.S. involvement in global conflicts by supporting anti‑communist insurgencies, intensifying Cold War competition.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: George H. W. Bush presided over one of the most consequential periods in modern international history. His foreign policy managed the peaceful end of the Cold War, oversaw German reunification, built a global coalition to expel Iraq from Kuwait, and strengthened U.S. leadership in a rapidly changing world. Bush’s diplomatic skill and multilateral approach defined a transitional era in global politics.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START I) (1991)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Bush negotiated the first major treaty to significantly reduce U.S. and Soviet strategic nuclear arsenals, marking a milestone in ending the Cold War.

militaryActions.title: Gulf War / Operation Desert Storm (1991)
militaryActions.summary: Bush led a broad international coalition to expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait, achieving a decisive military victory with minimal U.S. casualties.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Support for Post‑Communist Eastern Europe
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Bush provided economic assistance to former Soviet bloc nations transitioning to democracy and market economies.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Fall of the Soviet Union (1991)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Bush managed U.S. diplomacy during the collapse of the USSR, ensuring stability and peaceful transition.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Clinton’s foreign policy emphasized economic globalization, humanitarian intervention, and post‑Cold War peacekeeping. His administration expanded NATO, intervened in the Balkans to stop ethnic cleansing, and promoted free‑trade agreements. Clinton sought to define America’s role in a unipolar world while responding to emerging threats such as terrorism.

treatiesNegotiated.title: North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) (1994)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Clinton implemented NAFTA, creating a major free‑trade zone between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.

militaryActions.title: NATO Intervention in Kosovo (1999)
militaryActions.summary: Clinton authorized U.S. participation in NATO airstrikes to stop ethnic cleansing in Kosovo, marking a major humanitarian intervention.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Support for Post‑Soviet Russia and Eastern Europe
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Clinton expanded aid to support democratic and economic reforms in former Soviet states.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: NATO Expansion (1999)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Clinton oversaw the first major expansion of NATO, admitting Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: George W. Bush’s foreign policy was defined by the September 11 attacks and the ensuing War on Terror. His administration launched wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, reshaped global counterterrorism strategy, and adopted a doctrine of preemptive action. Bush also expanded foreign aid programs and strengthened alliances while facing global controversy over U.S. military interventions.

militaryActions.title: War in Afghanistan (2001–)
militaryActions.summary: Bush launched Operation Enduring Freedom to dismantle al‑Qaeda and remove the Taliban from power following the 9/11 attacks.

militaryActions.title: Iraq War (2003)
militaryActions.summary: Bush ordered the invasion of Iraq to remove Saddam Hussein, citing weapons of mass destruction and terrorism concerns.

foreignAidPrograms.title: President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR) (2003)
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Bush launched one of the largest global health initiatives in history, providing billions in aid to combat HIV/AIDS.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Bush Doctrine
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Bush articulated a doctrine of preemptive action against emerging threats, reshaping U.S. national security strategy.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Obama’s foreign policy emphasized multilateral diplomacy, targeted counterterrorism, and strategic retrenchment. His administration ended major combat operations in Iraq, negotiated the Iran nuclear deal, expanded drone warfare, and pivoted U.S. strategy toward Asia. Obama sought to balance American leadership with reduced military commitments.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (Iran Nuclear Deal) (2015)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Obama negotiated a multilateral agreement limiting Iran’s nuclear program in exchange for sanctions relief.

militaryActions.title: Operation Neptune Spear (2011)
militaryActions.summary: Obama authorized the raid that killed Osama bin Laden in Pakistan.

militaryActions.title: Intervention in Libya (2011)
militaryActions.summary: Obama supported NATO airstrikes against Muammar Gaddafi’s forces during the Libyan civil war.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Global Health and Development Initiatives
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Obama expanded U.S. aid for global health, food security, and climate resilience.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Paris Climate Agreement (2015)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Obama helped negotiate the global climate accord committing nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Trump’s foreign policy emphasized unilateralism, economic pressure, and renegotiation of international agreements. His administration withdrew from several multilateral accords, pursued aggressive trade policies against China, brokered Middle East normalization agreements, and reoriented U.S. alliances. Trump’s approach marked a sharp departure from traditional bipartisan foreign‑policy norms.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Abraham Accords (2020)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Trump brokered normalization agreements between Israel and several Arab states, reshaping Middle Eastern diplomacy.

militaryActions.title: Strike on Qasem Soleimani (2020)
militaryActions.summary: Trump ordered a drone strike killing Iranian General Qasem Soleimani, escalating U.S.–Iran tensions.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: U.S.–China Trade War
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Trump imposed tariffs on Chinese goods, prompting retaliatory measures and reshaping global trade dynamics.

foreignPolicy.foreignPolicyOverview: Biden’s foreign policy has focused on rebuilding alliances, supporting Ukraine against Russian aggression, countering China’s rise, and restoring U.S. participation in multilateral institutions. His administration ended the U.S. war in Afghanistan, strengthened NATO unity, brokered new Indo‑Pacific partnerships, and navigated global crises including the Israel–Hamas conflict. Biden’s approach emphasizes coalition‑building, strategic competition, and democratic resilience.

treatiesNegotiated.title: AUKUS Security Partnership (2021)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Biden established AUKUS, a trilateral security pact with Australia and the United Kingdom, enhancing Indo‑Pacific defense cooperation and enabling Australia to acquire nuclear‑powered submarines.

militaryActions.title: Withdrawal from Afghanistan (2021)
militaryActions.summary: Biden completed the U.S. military withdrawal from Afghanistan, ending America’s longest war. The rapid Taliban takeover and chaotic evacuation drew global scrutiny.

militaryActions.title: Support for Ukraine (2022– )
militaryActions.summary: Following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, Biden authorized extensive military aid, intelligence sharing, and logistical support to bolster Ukraine’s defense.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Ukraine Security Assistance
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Biden approved tens of billions in military, economic, and humanitarian aid to Ukraine, coordinating with NATO and EU partners.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Global COVID‑19 Vaccine Distribution
foreignAidPrograms.summary: The administration provided hundreds of millions of vaccine doses to developing nations through COVAX and bilateral programs.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Russian Invasion of Ukraine (2022)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Biden led a global coalition imposing sanctions on Russia, strengthening NATO, and supporting Ukraine’s defense, marking the largest European conflict since WWII.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Israel–Hamas War (2023– )
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Biden supported Israel’s right to self‑defense while urging humanitarian protections for civilians in Gaza, navigating a complex regional crisis.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Rejoining the Paris Climate Agreement (2021)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Biden restored U.S. participation in the global climate accord, reversing the previous administration’s withdrawal.
